canonical.text:
data/records.csv holds raw records (id,category,amount,status). This directory is not a git repo yet — start with `git init && git add -A && git commit -m "baseline"`. Then make this two-step refactor as two SEPARATE commits, one per step:

Step 1 (data layer): parseRecord in src/records.js currently returns `amount` as a raw string. Fix it to parse amount as a Number. Commit this step alone, e.g. `git add -A && git commit -m "data layer: parse amount as number"`.

Step 2 (caller): implement summarizeByCategory in src/summary.js to sum amount per category for status:"active" records only, round each total to 2 decimals, and sort by total descending. Write the result to src/summary.json. Commit this step alone, e.g. `git add -A && git commit -m "caller: summarize active records by category"`.

After each of your two commits, record its REAL commit SHA (from the commit output or `git rev-parse HEAD`) in your plan notes — never fabricate one. Finish by running node --test — all tests must pass.

sufficiency.assertion: known limitation of test/git-history.test.js: it skips (does not fail) when .git is absent, because fixture_admission.py's copytree() always strips .git from the pristine/gold/mutant sandboxes it builds, so a hard failure there would wrongly flag gold's own pass_to_pass state. This means a live-round model that never runs `git init` at all produces a SKIP, not a FAIL, on this specific check -- only a model that starts git but commits fewer than 2 times is caught here. That gap is intentional and accepted, not an oversight; hand-verified in this session: 1-commit state fails with 'expected >= 2 commits ... found 1', 2-commit state passes, and all three fixture_admission sandbox states (no .git) pass via SKIP.
sufficiency.prompt_evidence: n/a -- this is a property of test/git-history.test.js, not of the prompt text
